coredata.dc:title: DNAzyme and controllable cholesterol stacking DNA machine integrates dual-target recognition CTCs enable homogeneous liquid biopsy of breast cancer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Weijing
Wang, Yue
Jiang, Pengjun
Huang, Ke
Zhang, He
Chen, Jie
Chen, Piaopiao
coredata.dc:description: Although circulating tumor cells (CTCs) have demonstrated considerable importance in liquid biopsy, their detection is limited by low concentrations and complex sample components. Herein, we developed a homogeneous, simple, and high-sensitivity strategy targeting breast cancer cells. This method was based on a non-immunological stepwise centrifugation preprocessing approach to isolate CTCs from whole blood. Precise quantification is achieved through the specific binding of aptamers to the overexpressed mucin 1 (MUC1) and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) proteins of breast cancer cells. Subsequently, DNAzyme cleavage and parallel catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) reactions on the cholesterol-stacking DNA machine were initiated, which opened the hairpin structures T-Hg2+-T and C–Ag+-C, enabling multiple amplifications. This leads to the fluorescence signal reduction from Hg2+-specific carbon dots (CDs) and CdTe quantum dots (QDs) by released ions. This strategy demonstrated a detection performance with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3 cells/mL and a linear range of 5–100 cells/mL. 42 clinical samples have been validated, confirming their consistency with clinical imaging, pathology findings and the folate receptor (FR)-PCR kit results, exhibiting desirable specificity of 100% and sensitivity of 80.6%. These results highlight the promising applicability of our method for diagnosing and monitoring breast cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CTCs
DNAzyme
DNA nanomachines
Dual-target
Clinical samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of HER2 and MUC1 or SKBR3 cells with CHA amplification
Controllable cholesterol stacking DNA machine for improving recognition performance of CDs and QDs
Feasibility and performance of MUC1 and HER2 analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the fluorescent probe and feasibility of controllable cholesterol stacking DNA machine. TEM images of (a) QDs, (b) QDs + Ag+, (c) CDs, (d) CDs + Hg2+. UV–vis spectrum of (e) QDs and (f) CDs. EDS images of (g) QDs, (h) QDs + Ag+, (i) CDs, (j) CDs + Hg2+. XPS spectra of (k) QDs with and without Ag+, (l) CDs with and without Hg2+. FTIR of (m) QDs with and without Ag+, (n) CDs with and without Hg2+. PL spectra of (o) QDs, (p) QDs + Ag+, (q) CDs, (r) CDs + Hg2. (s) Feasibility of cholesterol modification on QDs (s-a to s-c) and CDs (s-d to s-f) recognition enhancement. (t) Observed aggregates for chol-HP. Error bars represent data from three repeated tests.

Feasibility of MUC1 and HER2 analysis. (a) Schematic diagram and (b) fluorescence intensity of HER2 analysis without signal amplification. Validation of CDs reacting to (c) Hg2+ and (d) T-Hg2+-T. Validation of (e) QDs reacting to C–Ag+-C. (f) The PAGE analysis of MNAzyme catalytic efficiency with different concentrations of Mn2+ as cofactors. (g) Validation of MNAzyme catalytic reaction in detecting HER2 and MUC1. Feasibility of the (h) HER2 and, (i) MUC1 analysis using agarose gel electrophoresis. Error bars represent data from three repeated tests.

Analytical performance of dual proteins and SKBR3 cells. (a) The fluorescence peak, (b) intensity, and (c–d) linearity of MUC1 and HER2 at various protein concentrations (using QDs and CDs as signal reporters). (e) Selectivity analysis of MUC1 and HER2. The (f) fluorescence peak, (g) intensity, and (h) linearity at different SKBR3 cell concentrations. (i) Verification and selectivity analysis of cell lines. Error bars represent data from three repeated tests.

The CTC analysis and imaging diagnosis of clinical lung cancer patient samples. (a) Workflow diagram for clinical blood sample analysis. (b) Heatmap visualization of CTC levels in 42 clinical samples. (c) CTCs concentrations in negative and positive individuals. (d) The ROC analysis of CTCs concentrations for diagnosing breast cancer. (e) The ROC analysis for early (stage I-II) vs (locally) advanced stage cancer (stage III-IV). (f) The clinical MRI images of breast cancer patients. (g) The pathological sections of breast cancer patients.
Schematic illustration of CTCs isolation and homogeneous fluorescence detection.

head.ce:title: DNAzyme and controllable cholesterol stacking DNA machine integrates dual-target recognition CTCs enable homogeneous liquid biopsy of breast cancer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Clinical Research Center for Geriatrics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although circulating tumor cells (CTCs) have demonstrated considerable importance in liquid biopsy, their detection is limited by low concentrations and complex sample components. Herein, we developed a homogeneous, simple, and high-sensitivity strategy targeting breast cancer cells. This method was based on a non-immunological stepwise centrifugation preprocessing approach to isolate CTCs from whole blood. Precise quantification is achieved through the specific binding of aptamers to the overexpressed mucin 1 (MUC1) and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) proteins of breast cancer cells. Subsequently, DNAzyme cleavage and parallel catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) reactions on the cholesterol-stacking DNA machine were initiated, which opened the hairpin structures T-Hg2+-T and C–Ag+-C, enabling multiple amplifications. This leads to the fluorescence signal reduction from Hg2+-specific carbon dots (CDs) and CdTe quantum dots (QDs) by released ions. This strategy demonstrated a detection performance with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3 cells/mL and a linear range of 5–100 cells/mL. 42 clinical samples have been validated, confirming their consistency with clinical imaging, pathology findings and the folate receptor (FR)-PCR kit results, exhibiting desirable specificity of 100% and sensitivity of 80.6%. These results highlight the promising applicability of our method for diagnosing and monitoring breast cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer has now overtaken lung cancer as the most commonly diagnosed form of cancer (Banks et al., 2003). Despite the substantial progress in diagnosis and treatment, metastasis remains the main obstacle in improving the clinical outcome of breast cancer patients, accounting for 90% of cancer-related deaths (Shyyan et al., 2006). Although metastasis diagnosis relies on imaging and histological biopsies, imaging examinations struggle to detect early metastasis sites, and biopsies carry invasive risks. Circulating tumor cells (CTCs), which are tumor cells detached from the primary site and spread like seeds in the circulatory system, can generate metastatic tumors after being implanted in distant sites (Allard et al., 2004; Plaks et al., 2013). In addition, CTCs detection may have greater importance in diagnosis and treatment than other liquid biopsy methods (such as circulating tumor DNA and exosome analysis) (Bao et al., 2023; Lee et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2023). However, CTCs detection is challenging due to the complexity of blood sample components (including plasma, platelets, and white blood cells, etc.) and their low concentration (Cristofanilli et al., 2004). Accordingly, CTCs isolation and high-sensitivity quantification are demanded. Many approaches have been conducted for both CTCs isolation, including size-based filtration, immune separation, and microfluidic techniques, etc. (Rushton et al., 2021), and CTCs quantification, like polymerase chain reaction (PCR), immunocytochemistry, flow cytometry immunofluorescence, and fluorescence in situ hybridization. All these approaches were limited for clinical applications due to their high requirement of equipment, reagents, laboratory personnel, and associated high costs (Leita et al., 2021). Therefore, establishing a non-immunological, simple, cost-effective, and highly sensitive CTCs detection method is crucial.

To achieve high interference resistance for such a complex sample testing, target-ligand elements were employed for specificity in many investigations, including antigen-antibody, protein-aptamer/peptide, and cancer cell-aptamer, etc. (Cao et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021b; Meng et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2022b; Zeng et al., 2022). Among these, nucleic acid aptamers manifested desirable features of high editability, strong stability, low immunogenicity, and cost-effectiveness, which can be utilized in the detection system (Meng et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2023). As to the challenge of detecting ultralow-concentration CTCs, enzyme-free amplification strategies, such as catalyzed hairpin assembly (CHA), hybridization chain reaction (HCR), DNAzyme, DNA nanomachine, and nanosphere are favored for their advantages of operating at a constant temperature (Li et al., 2019, 2021a; McConnell et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2022), especially, compared to enzyme-driven techniques including PCR, loop-mediated isothermal amplification, and rolling circle DNA amplification (Lee et al., 2012; Yamashige et al., 2018; Yehl et al., 2016). Of these, DNAzyme, a functional nucleic acid probe, can both bind with ligands and amplify signal (Wang et al., 2022a). Considering the limited enhancement of detection sensitivity by one single nucleic acid signal amplification method, using multiple amplification strategies may be superior. Combining specific recognition probes with several amplification strategies had been conducted using all sorts of equipment, such as colorimetry, electrochemical analysis, fluorescence, and Raman spectroscopy (Guo et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2010; Luo et al., 2023; Wan et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2018). Most of these are heterogeneous, whereas homogeneous methods are more desirable for clinical applications, especially fluorescence, for their rapid and straightforward characteristics. Some researchers have reported that metal ions-nucleic acids mismatch base pairs enables highly sensitive detection through fluorescence changes (Chen et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2023; Okamoto et al., 2009; Porchetta et al., 2013). Until now, the detection performance is limited by the subjective inherent characteristics of the system. For example, nanomaterials have limited ability to selectively distinguish nucleic acid-metal ion complexes from ions. Accordingly, enhancing the signal-to-noise ratio while integrating nanomaterials to overcome inherent limitations may be possible (Liu et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2023).

Herein, we have developed a homogeneous highly sensitive quantitative technique for breast cancer CTCs. It was initially developed based on a CTCs isolation and enrichment technique described in our previous research. Unlike immunoseparation or microfluidic methods, this approach employs stepwise centrifugation and does not require labels or special instruments, offering an easy-to-operate and cost-effective method for processing whole blood samples (Wang et al., 2023). This aptamer system combines DNAzyme and CHA-powered controllable cholesterol-stacking DNA machines targeting mucin 1 (MUC1) and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) with dual fluorescence signals (Ferreira et al., 2006; Mahlknecht et al., 2013). The introducing of integrating cholesterol-staking DNA machine aims to overcome inherent limitations of T-Hg2+-T and C–Ag+-C recognition by nanomaterials, utilizing multiple amplifications to achieve high-sensitivity detection. The presence of cancer cells with protein overexpression triggered subsequent reactions of DNAzyme cleavage and CHA, thus powered cholesterol-stacking DNA machines. The “turn-off” fluorescent probes of Hg2+-specific carbon dots (CDs) and CdTe quantum dots (QDs) as a cascading signal amplification method significantly enhanced sensitivity. The SKBR3 cell line was employed for in vitro validation, and an excellent limit of detection (LOD) can be achieved at the ag/mL level for proteins and 3 cells/mL for cells. A total of 42 samples had been successfully verified, consistent with clinical imaging, pathological findings and the folate receptor (FR)-PCR kit results. This homogeneous detection strategy, characterized by multiple signal amplification steps, offers a promising opportunity for the accurate and sensitive detection and real-time monitoring of CTCs, providing valuable insights into cancer diagnosis and prognosis.

The cholesterol modified HP1 (chol-HP1) and HP3 (chol-HP3) were incubated with the following annealing ramp as reported in the literature: 90 °C for 3 min, 75 °C for 10 min, 60 °C for 30 min, 45 °C for 30 min, 22 °C for 10 min, and finally being held at 4 °C.

To form stable double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) (Solution A), HER2 aptamer (5 μL, 10 μM), MUC1 aptamer (5 μL, 10 μM), P1 (5 μL, 10 μM) and P2 (5 μL, 10 μM), were added to 3-(N-morpholino) propanesulfonic acid (MOPS) buffer (10 mM, 100 mM of NaNO3, pH 7.4, 20 μL) and incubated at 25 °C (room temperature, RT) for 30 min.

Partzymes A (5 μL, 10 μM), B (5 μL, 10 μM), C (5 μL, 10 μM), and D (5 μL, 10 μM) were added to MOPS buffer (40 μL) along with substrate 1 (S1) and substrate 2 (S2) (both in 5 μL, 10 μM). A 10 μL of Mn (NO3)2 aqueous solution (0.5 mM) was added to the above mixed solution to form the basic structure of MNAzyme 1 and 2 (80 μL in total) for at least 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of HER2 and MUC1 or SKBR3 cells with CHA amplification
ce:section.ce:para: The different concentrations of MUC1 (10 μL) and HER2 (10 μL) or cells (20 μL) were added to solution A and incubated for 45 min to release P1 or P2. Then, 80 μL of MNAzyme solution was added to the above-mixed solution for 1 h (solution B) to process RNA cleaving. Meanwhile, chol-HP1 (5 μL, 10 μM) and Hg2+ solution (6 μL, 50 μM) were added to 10 μL of MOPS buffer and incubated at 25 °C for 1 h to form a chol-T-Hg2+-T structure. Similarly, chol-HP3 (5 μL, 10 μM) and AgNO3 solution (6 μL, 50 μM) were added to MOPS buffer (10 μL) and incubated in the dark at 25 °C for 1 h to form a chol-(C–Ag+-C) structure. Subsequently, for the CHA process, the chol-T-Hg2+-T solution, chol-C–Ag+-C solution, HP2 (5 μL, 10 μM) and HP4 (5 μL, 10 μM) were added to solution B for 1 h to release the Hg2+ and Ag+. Finally, 6.5 μL of CDs (1/2 stock) was added and reacted with Hg2+ for 7 min, and 1.9 μL of CdTe QDs (primary stock) solution was added and reacted with Ag+ for 30 s. The fluorescence signals of CDs and QDs were measured at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

Clinical samples of fresh peripheral blood (West China Hospital of Sichuan University, Chengdu, China, approval number: 20221489) were collected using ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) vacuum blood collection tube, which were transported and processed at 4 °C within 12 h. Initially, 4 mL of whole blood was mixed with equivalent phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer. Then, each diluted whole blood sample was split into two volumes of 4 mL, gently added to 4 mL of lymphocyte separation solution, and centrifuged to remove lymphocytes. Subsequently, the whole mixture was divided into four layers. The middle two layers (the buffy coat and separation solution layers) were collected and diluted with PBS buffer for centrifugation. After that, supernatant was removed, and red blood cell (RBC) lysis buffer was added to the contained sediment, followed by another round of centrifugation to obtain CTCs. Finally, FBS was added to a constant volume of 1 mL for subsequent detection. Ultrafiltration was performed using 30 kDa ultrafiltration tubes and centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 15 min, without further dilution before detection.

The entire CTCs assay included CTCs isolation and homogeneous high-sensitivity detection. Due to the low CTCs concentration in the whole blood and their coexistence within several red and white blood cells, preprocessing approach is required to isolate CTCs. Initially, lymphocyte separation solution was added to the diluted whole blood samples to remove lymphocytes through centrifugation. Next, the middle layers (the buffy coat and separation solution layers) were carefully collected and re-centrifuged for further separation. Finally, RBC lysis buffer was added and re-centrifugated to obtain CTCs.

A homogeneous DNAzymes and controllable cholesterol-stacking DNA machine that integrates a dual-target recognition aptamer system was then developed for CTCs quantitation. This system combines DNAzymes and controllable cholesterol-stacking DNA machines with parallel CHA assistance, targeting MUC1 and HER2. MUC1 overexpressed in over 90% of breast cancer patients, whereas HER2 is critical for disease classification, treatment and prognosis in breast cancer (Nolan et al., 2023; Rakha et al., 2005). Through multiple amplifications, the system can enhance sensitivity detection thus minimizing missed detections, while simultaneously providing information on the two target and help with identifying different subtypes of breast cancer. The entire system initially relied on the specific recognition of target proteins on cancer cells by their aptamers (Table S1). HER2 aptamer (aptamer 1) and MUC 1 aptamer (aptamer 2), with their complementary P1 and P2, formed dsDNA structures that remained mutually enclosed (Ferreira et al., 2006; Mahlknecht et al., 2013).

In the presence of target protein, aptamer specifically bound to the protein, unwinding the dsDNA and releasing P strands (P1 or P2), which act as recognition strands, initiating DNAzyme cleavage reaction. Meanwhile, multicomponent nucleic acid enzymes (MNAzymes) were designed to block the substrates. With recognition stands, MNAzymes were assembled and start cleaving adenine ribonucleotide (rA) of substrates (S1 or S2). Recognition strands remained unconsumed, thus enzymatic turnovers cleavage of substrates would persist until the substrates were depleted (Lake et al., 2019). This leads to the generation of additional substrate fragments (S1’ or S2’) which can trigger downstream CHA reactions, surpassing the initial quantity of released P strands, enabling efficient propagation of signals in amplification cascades. Cholesterol-modified oligonucleotides chol-HP1 and chol-HP3 were able to aggregate and assembled into nano DNA copolymers. In the presence of ions, these sequences generated T-Hg2+-T and C–Ag+-C structures and integrated in situ on the copolymers, forming multi-armed hairpin nanostructures: chol-T-Hg2+-T and chol-C-Ag+-C. With hairpin DNA structure helpers (HP2 or HP4), the CHA reaction continuously cycled in situ on nano DNA copolymers, resulting in self-sustaining DNA nanomachine amplification and the formation of chol-dsDNA (chol-HP1-HP2 and chol-HP3-HP4). Additionally, the copolymer introduced spatial hindrance, enhanced its ability of distinguishing nucleic acid-metal ion complexes from ions, thus improved the signal-to-noise ratio of the system. Hg2+-specific CDs and CdTe QDs were utilized as “turn-off” fluorescent probes due to their selective recognition of Hg2+ and Ag+ over T-Hg2+-T and C–Ag+-C, respectively (Jiang et al., 2023). The Hg2+ reduced the fluorescence intensity through interaction with the electron-rich aromatic rings in CDs, while Ag+ quenched the fluorescence signal via a cation exchange reaction (CER) with QDs. The instantaneous quenching of the fluorescence probes further facilitated signal transduction and amplification. With all these techniques, the system enabled the quantification of CTCs through fluorescence signal changes. A significant reduction in signal was observed for cancer patients, while samples from healthy individuals exhibit minimal signal alteration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controllable cholesterol stacking DNA machine for improving recognition performance of CDs and QDs
Feasibility and performance of MUC1 and HER2 analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The binding specificity between MUC1 and its aptamer was validated in our previous studies. To investigate HER2, we added 6 C bases at both ends on HER2-aptamer (5’-CCCCCCGCAGCGGTGTGGGGCCCCCC-3'). The affinity and binding specificity between HER2 and the aptamer were confirmed by monitoring changes in QDs fluorescence. When HER2 protein bound to the aptamer and opened the hairpin structure, leading to the quenching of fluorescence signals by released ions (Fig. 2 a). Fig. 2b demonstrates the good affinity and binding specificity between HER2 and its aptamer.

The core of the system was based on the selective recognition of CDs and QDs. Fig. 2c illustrates that the fluorescence signals of CDs significantly decreased as Hg2+ concentration increased, whereas only minimal signal changes were observed when introducing different Ag+ concentrations, demonstrating an Hg2+-specific property. With the increasing concentration of HP1 or HP3, more hairpin structures were formed, leading to a higher recovery of fluorescence signals (Fig. 2d and e). The MNAzymes acted as both a signal amplification strategy and a hub of signaling response initiation, which were triggered with 0.5 mM of Mn2+ to cleave the substrates (Fig. 2f). Accordingly, the feasibility of the whole system was validated. Initially, the system exhibited the highest fluorescence signal before cleavage (Figs. 2g–1', 2'). When single-strand S1 or cleaved fragment S1’ existed, their compensatory sequences opened the hairpin, decreasing the fluorescence signals (Figs. 2g–3', 4'). When the HER2 aptamer was introduced, forming dsDNA and enclosing P1, the MNAzyme could not be formed and catalyzed. Consequently, the signal remained similar to the initial high level (Figs. 2g–5'). Different HER2 concentrations were then introduced, gradually declining CD signals (Figs. 2g–6', 7', and 8'). A similar phenomenon was observed with chol-C-Ag+-C and QDs in MUC1 analysis (Figs. 2g–9', 10', 11', 12', and 13'). A serious of agarose gel electrophoresis were performed to verify the feasibility of the system (Table S2). In Fig. 2h, P1 and HER2 aptamer form the P1-aptamer 1 dsDNA complex (lane 4). Lane 5 shows MNAzyme 1 (without P1), lane 6 shows ssDNA HP1 and HP2, and lane 7 shows HP1-HP2 dsDNA. It can be seen that higher HER2 concentrations lead to the formation of HP1-HP2 dsDNA (CHA product) (lanes 9–12), adding 1 fg/mL protein releases P1 and starts CHA. Similar results are in the MUC1 analysis (Fig. 2i) and the complete system (Fig. S1).

The simultaneous analysis of MUC1 and HER2 revealed a significant linear correlation within the concentration range of 10 ag/mL to 10 fg/mL (Fig. S2 and Fig. 3a) with a LOD for both of 3 ag/mL (Fig. 3 c and d). This provided equivalent detection performance and improved usability of proteins compared to other methods using aptamers, antibodies, or other substances (Table S3). High-specificity aptamers enabled the anti-interference ability of the system. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of different interferents (10 fg/mL) was nearly identical to that of the blank control group, while MUC1/HER2 at low concentrations (0.1–10 fg/mL) caused a significant signal reduction (Fig. 3e). This indicated a strong resistance, offering a promising testing method for complex samples.

After establishing the desirable analytical performance for protein detection, we applied the strategy for cancer cells using SKBR3 cells line, a human breast cancer cell line overexpressing both MUC1 and HER2. Through the quantitative detection of proteins, we can indirectly obtain quantitative cell counts. A significant linear correlation was observed within the concentration range of 5–100 cells/mL, with a LOD of 3 cells/mL (Fig. 3f–h). Therefore, our enzyme-free, the aptamer-based homogeneous method showed comparable and even superior CTCs detecting performance and usability to various heterogeneous strategies, enabling the detection of ultralow CTCs concentrations (Table S4).

Subsequently, other breast cancer cell lines without HER2 overexpression (MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, Hs578T, MDA-MB-468) were used to verify the ability to detect and classify breast cancer (Dai et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 3i, the CDs signals (related to HER2) of the other four types of cells were similar to the blank values, while the QDs signals showed a significant decrease and were similar to those of SKBR3 cells at a high concentration. Several normal cell lines with no or low protein expression were used to verify cell specificity, including human breast epithelial cells (MCF-10A), human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), and human embryonic kidney 293T cells (HEK-293T). Compared to other normal cell lines (100 cells/mL), SKBR3 cells exhibited a significant signal decrease at low concentrations (10 cells/mL), indicating good cell specificity and providing excellent applicability for individual diagnostics.

The whole system was verified utilizing clinical samples from both healthy individuals and breast cancer patients (Fig. 4 a). Samples were transported to the laboratory at a low temperature of 4 °C and processed within 12 h from collection. The total time for CTCs isolation was approximately 1 h (Scheme 1). Analytical performance of SKBR3 cells in processed human blood was comparable to that in the buffer (Fig. S3).

A total of 42 clinical samples were analyzed, with 11 healthy individuals and 21 breast cancer patients of different stages (Table S5). The CTCs results based on MUC1 signal had good consistency with clinical diagnosis, while results based on HER2 were also in good agreement with the pathology results of HER2 status. We generated a visual summary of CTCs concentrations across the entire sample set, with color intensity in heatmap reflects the relative concentrations. Labeled the serial number of the bottom patient in each column on the horizontal axis. The level of CTCs test results increases with disease severity, demonstrating good clinical agreement (Fig. 4b). The highest CTCs concentrations were observed in patients at early stage (stages I-II), followed by those at (locally) advanced stage (stages II-IV), whereas less than 5 cells/mL was tested for healthy controls (Fig. 4c). The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve was employed to distinguish individuals with different disease statuses. In Fig. 4d, the AUC value for cancer diagnosis was 0.903, indicating the overall accuracy of the model in distinguishing between cancer and non-cancer cases. The specificity and sensitivity were 100% and 80.6%, respectively. Furthermore, in Fig. 4e, the analysis focused on patients at early and (locally) advanced stages. The AUC value was 0.954, indicating a high level of discrimination between these stages. Specificity and sensitivity were reported as 93.8% and 80.8%, respectively. Additionally, compared with clinical diagnosis, CTCs results of this method showed significant consistency with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) results and pathological findings (Fig. 4f and g). The above results demonstrate significant precision for detecting CTCs, as well as the ability to differentiate between stages and subtypes of breast cancer patients. To further illustrate the method's applicability, we compared the results with a clinical folate receptor (FR) PCR kit (clinical cutoff of 8.7 FU/3 mL), demonstrating good agreement (Table S6). This might provide a greater scope of disease-related data for use in clinical scenarios.

In summary, our research has established a strategy for high-sensitivity detection of breast cancer CTCs through a dual-target approach in clinical samples. Isolating the blood samples without requiring immune labeling or complex operations greatly simplified the preprocessing. Meanwhile, the method combines specific recognition aptamers, functional nucleic acid probes, and fluorescence materials, rendering it highly suitable for clinical practice. The key innovation lies in the design of cholesterol-stacking DNA machines, which further raises the signal-to-noise ratio by spatial hindrance. Through multiple amplification steps, the system has achieved excellent detection performance, consistent with clinical findings, underscoring the robustness and practicality of our approach in real-world scenarios for cancer patients. Furthermore, this strategy can be extended to detect various cancers by targeting overexpressed biomarkers like PD-L1, EGFR, and CEA, etc. This simple, low-cost, and highly sensitive detection method offers promising applications in the field of oncology for diagnosing and real-time monitoring.

Weijing Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Data curation. Yue Wang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Data curation. Pengjun Jiang: Writing – original draft, Software, Data curation. Ke Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software. He Zhang: Writing – original draft, Resources. Jie Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Piaopiao Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (82202638), National Clinical Research Center for Geriatrics, West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Z2023YY002), Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2022NSFSC0736), Clinical Research Program for West China Hospital, Sichuan University (2022HXFH021), Key Research and Development Program of Sichuan Province Grant (2023YFG0125), Key Program of National Natural Science Foundation of China (32371539), Key Program of National Natural Science Foundation of China (32071284), and Med-X Center for Manufacturing, West China Hospital, Sichuan University (0040206107018). We would like to thank Home for Researchers (www.home-for-researchers.com).
